hook: You already know how to describe the world around you — a dot on a page, a straight road, a flat tabletop. But geometry needs a precise, shared language so that everyone means exactly the same thing when they say 'line' or 'angle.' What if mastering just a handful of foundational ideas could unlock every geometric proof and figure you'll ever encounter?
concept: This unit builds the essential vocabulary and measurement tools of geometry — everything from naming the simplest figures to calculating size and shape — unified by the idea that all of geometry rests on a small set of carefully defined building blocks, known formally as the axiomatic foundation of Euclidean geometry.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Undefined Terms: Points, Lines, and Planes
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn how points, lines, and planes serve as the starting building blocks of all geometry — figures so fundamental they are described rather than formally defined.
You will learn to distinguish between segments, rays, and angles, and use precise notation to name and describe each one correctly.
Master how to calculate the length of a segment and the degree measure of an angle using the Ruler Postulate and Protractor Postulate.
Apply the concepts of bisectors to find midpoints and divide angles into two equal parts, setting up key relationships you'll use throughout geometry.
You will learn to classify angles as acute, right, obtuse, or straight, and identify special angle pairs — such as complementary, supplementary, and vertical angles — to solve for unknown measures.
Sharpen your ability to calculate perimeter, circumference, and area for common shapes, connecting measurement formulas to the geometric figures you've been naming and classifying.
You will learn to identify and describe common 3-D solids — such as prisms, pyramids, and spheres — extending your geometric vocabulary from flat figures into three dimensions.
